story.title: Dr. Lena Kowalski: Decoding Starlight
story.text:
My name is Dr. Lena Kowalski. I am a scientist at the Space Telescope Science Institute. I study starlight to map the universe, like a space detective.

When you look up at night, you see a star's apparent brightness. This is how bright it appears from Earth. But this can be tricky. A star might look dim simply because it is very far away. Or it could be closer but have a low actual brightness. That is the true amount of light a star produces.

My job is to solve this puzzle. First, I study a star’s color to classify it. This gives me a good estimate of its actual brightness. Then, I use powerful space telescopes, like Hubble, to measure its apparent brightness very carefully. We use space telescopes because Earth's atmosphere distorts starlight.

Knowing both brightnesses helps me calculate the distance. We measure these huge distances in light-years. The farther a star is, the less observable detail we can see. Every star I measure becomes another point on our map of the universe.
think_abouts: Why does Dr. Kowalski call herself a 'space detective'?
If two stars have the same actual brightness, what makes one look dimmer than the other?
story.go_deeper: How might knowing a star's distance help us learn about its age or the galaxy it is in?

margin_defs.definition: to find an answer using math

check_prompts: What are the two things that affect a star's apparent brightness?

vocabulary.definition: How bright a star LOOKS from Earth, which depends on distance.
vocabulary.use_sentence: A nearby dim star can have a higher apparent brightness than a faraway bright star.

vocabulary.definition: How much light a star truly produces, also called luminosity.
vocabulary.use_sentence: A giant blue star has a very high actual brightness.

vocabulary.definition: The distance light travels in one year — about 6 trillion miles.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The closest star system is over four light-years away from Earth.

vocabulary.definition: How much information we can see about a distant object.
vocabulary.use_sentence: With a better telescope, we can see more observable detail on distant planets.

discussion_cards.prompt: Imagine you discover two stars. Star A looks very bright. Star B looks very dim. What information do you need before you can say which star produces more light?
discussion_cards.frame: Before I can know which star has a higher actual brightness, I need to know ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Kowalski uses space telescopes to get a clearer view of stars. Why is a telescope in space better than one on the ground for her work?
discussion_cards.frame: A telescope in space is better because ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: If we see the light from a star that is 100 light-years away, what are we actually seeing?
discussion_cards.frame: When we see a star 100 light-years away, we are seeing light that is ___.

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
